signals: Chronic load baseline climbed steeply across the 57-day window, reaching period highs in early March (both EWMA chronic and Garmin-like rolling chronic).
Two clear “surge” blocks are visible (mid-February and early March), each marked by elevated ramp rate and uncoupled ACWR (acute relative to the prior-week baseline).

The most recent high week (ending 2026-03-08) produced the peak values for acute load, chronic load, monotony, and strain; 2026-03-09 (0 load) reduced short-term metrics but rolling 7d/28d measures remain elevated.
Training stimulus has been volatile: frequent true-zero days interspersed with large single-day loads, producing large day-to-day deltas and periodic spike indicators.
VO2 Max estimates show a step-down in running from 54 → ~51 in mid-February and relative stability at ~50–51 since; cycling VO2 Max has only 3 samples and shows a small drop (60 → 58).
evidence: Chronic EWMA (28d) rose from ~25.0 on 2026-01-12 to a max of 77.6 on 2026-03-08 (then 72.2 on 2026-03-09 after a 0-load day).
Garmin-like chronic baseline (chronic_28d_avg) increased from 240.8 on 2026-02-01 → 357.0 on 2026-02-28 → 483.0 on 2026-03-09 (period max), indicating a rapidly rising “weekly-load baseline.”
Weekly load (acute_7d_sum) peaks: 672.1 on 2026-02-15 and a higher max 798.0 on 2026-03-08 (then 624.8 on 2026-03-09).

Spike indicators (uncoupled): acwr_uncoupled peaked at 3.64 on 2026-02-15; it returned to ~2.0+ in early March (2.03 on 2026-03-02; 2.07 on 2026-03-08). Weekly uncoupled ACWR (acwr_7d28d_uncoupled) peaked at 2.63 on 2026-02-15 and 2.19 on 2026-03-08.
Monotony/strain: monotony_7d reached 2.34 and strain_7d 1866.8 on 2026-03-08 (period highs). For comparison, the prior big surge day 2026-02-15 had monotony_7d 1.19 and strain_7d 803.0.
TSB (chronic_ewma − acute_ewma) extremes: most negative -52.9 on 2026-01-14; large negative again -47.8 on 2026-02-15; recent -33.7 on 2026-03-08 improving to -11.3 on 2026-03-09.
Volatility markers: 14/57 days had daily_load = 0.0 (~25% of days). Largest single-day jump +220.5 (2026-02-14 → 2026-02-15) and largest drop -242.0 (2026-02-15 → 2026-02-16).

Training status snapshot shows VO2 max 51.0 dated 2026-03-08 and an undated acute/chronic load of 629/503 with ACWR 1.2; magnitude is consistent with nearby acute_7d_sum 624.8 (2026-03-09) and chronic_28d_avg 483.0 (2026-03-09).
implications: Quantitatively, base rebuilding is real and substantial: both chronic baselines (EWMA and rolling-sum) are at period highs by 2026-03-08/2026-03-09.
The biggest “stress reality” signal in early March is not just high weekly load, but the combination of high weekly load + high monotony, which is what drives the record strain value on 2026-03-08.

Coupled metrics (e.g., acwr_7d28d) can visually look less extreme than uncoupled versions during surge weeks; uncoupled ACWR provides a clearer spike flag when weekly load ramps quickly relative to the prior-week baseline.

VO2 Max estimates are currently behaving more like step changes + plateau (running ~51 since 2026-02-13) rather than a smooth rising trend; interpretation should be cautious given sparse sampling and algorithm dependence.
uncertainty: training_status.acute_training_load fields (acute_load/chronic_load/acwr) have no date; alignment is inferred from similarity to 2026-03-09 rolling-sum metrics, but the snapshot could be from a nearby date.
Early-window uncoupled ACWR outliers (e.g., 2026-01-12/2026-01-13) are dominated by an extremely low baseline (chronic_uncoupled ~0.8) and are not comparable to later “steady-baseline” ACWR values.
VO2 Max sampling is irregular (running: 14 points; cycling: 3 points) with gaps up to 20 days; short-term changes may reflect estimation noise or context rather than a stable underlying trend.
monotony_7d = 0.00 and strain_7d = 0.0 on 2026-01-23–2026-01-25 conflicts with non-zero daily_load/acute_7d_sum on 2026-01-25, suggesting a calculation edge-case when recent loads are near-zero.

signals: The athlete’s load capacity (as represented by chronic baselines) is rising quickly; by early March the system is operating at the highest chronic levels in this dataset.

Load progression has been achieved via pronounced surges (notably 2026-02-11→2026-02-17 and 2026-03-02→2026-03-08) rather than a smooth ramp, producing repeated spike flags in uncoupled ACWR and ramp_7d.
Recent strain is being driven disproportionately by monotony rising above 2.0 (a structural signal about how load is distributed across days), not only by weekly totals.
The data show that deload “resets” can rapidly normalize spike indicators (e.g., late Feb), suggesting that planned oscillation is already part of the athlete’s pattern (intentional or not).
evidence: Chronic_28d_avg trajectory (Garmin-like baseline of weekly load): 240.8 (2026-02-01) → 303.7 (2026-02-21) → 357.0 (2026-02-28) → 483.0 (2026-03-09). This is ~2× growth from early February to early March.
EWMA chronic (28d) increased from ~28–35 range in early February (e.g., 28.8 on 2026-02-08; 35.0 on 2026-02-09) to 77.6 by 2026-03-08.

Uncoupled ACWR (acute vs baseline from 7 days prior) hit high values during surges: 3.64 (2026-02-15), 2.03 (2026-03-02), 2.07 (2026-03-08). Uncoupled weekly ACWR (acwr_7d28d_uncoupled) similarly peaked at 2.63 (2026-02-15) and 2.19 (2026-03-08).

Strain escalation is largely monotony-driven in early March: strain_7d 1866.8 on 2026-03-08 with monotony_7d 2.34, versus strain_7d 803.0 on 2026-02-15 with monotony_7d 1.19 (weekly load was high on both dates: acute_7d_sum 798 vs 672).
“Deload signature” example: 2026-02-22 to 2026-02-23 acute_7d_sum = 219.4 with coupled ACWR ~0.70–0.71 and TSB positive (4.6 to 12.0), coinciding with a clear step-down from the prior high block.
implications: Treat the current March baseline (chronic_28d_avg ~480; EWMA chronic ~72–78) as a meaningful new platform for the next 12–24 weeks; future progression will be more sustainable if it builds from this platform rather than repeatedly doubling the prior-week baseline (uncoupled ACWR ≥ ~2).

The most limiting structural signal is the combination of: high ramp_7d + high uncoupled ACWR + high monotony (≥2). When all three align (e.g., 2026-03-07 to 2026-03-08), strain reaches extreme values for this dataset.

Given the competition calendar (C race 2026-03-28; B race 2026-04-18; A race 2026-05-30), the load architecture opportunity is to prioritize consistency and controlled oscillation (planned deloads) so that chronic baselines remain high without relying on “shock weeks” that force large recovery troughs.

From a planning perspective, use uncoupled metrics (acwr_uncoupled and acwr_7d28d_uncoupled) as primary spike detectors, because coupled metrics can understate surge magnitude when the chronic baseline is being pulled upward by the surge itself.
uncertainty: This dataset covers 57 continuous days; it’s enough to detect patterns but not enough to confidently define an individual long-term “maximum sustainable” chronic load ceiling across the whole season.

Garmin load units are device- and ecosystem-dependent; if activity types or recording behaviors change (e.g., more cycling/strength logged vs not logged), year-to-year comparisons of absolute load can shift.

Long-term VO2 Max trend has apparent outliers (e.g., running 66 on 2025-11-17; cycling 68 on 2025-10-26) and large sampling gaps, limiting confidence in using VO2 Max trends as a season-long KPI from this data alone.

signals: You are immediately post a very high-load, high-strain week ending 2026-03-08; 2026-03-09 (0 load) reduced acute load and improved TSB, but 7d/28d rolling metrics remain elevated by design.

Short-term spike indicators are improving day-over-day (uncoupled ACWR fell from ~2.07 on 2026-03-08 to 1.35 on 2026-03-09), but the last 7 days still represent an above-baseline week (coupled ACWR 1.29; uncoupled weekly ACWR 1.65).

Monotony-driven strain is the key near-term guardrail: monotony reached >2.0 on 2026-03-07 and 2026-03-08 and strain exceeded 1500 on both days, which is exceptional relative to the rest of the dataset.

The load system is still in “build mode” (chronic_28d_avg continuing to rise to a max on 2026-03-09), meaning even steady training can keep baseline rising as low values roll out of the 28-day window.
evidence: Latest day (2026-03-09): daily_load 0.0; acute_ewma 83.5; chronic_ewma 72.2; TSB -11.3; ramp_7d 10.3; acute_7d_sum 624.8; chronic_28d_avg 483.0; coupled ACWR 1.29; monotony_7d 1.60; strain_7d 998.6; acwr_uncoupled 1.35; acwr_7d28d_uncoupled 1.65.

Peak stress day (2026-03-08): acute_7d_sum 798.0 (period max); chronic_28d_avg 471.8; coupled ACWR 1.69; monotony_7d 2.34 (period max); strain_7d 1866.8 (period max); TSB -33.7; ramp_7d 23.9; acwr_uncoupled 2.07; acwr_7d28d_uncoupled 2.19.
Recent surge ramp context: ramp_7d stayed elevated 2026-03-02→2026-03-08 (18.1, 11.7, 17.6, 18.2, 10.0, 19.7, 23.9), indicating persistent fast growth in EWMA chronic over that week.

Deload comparison point (late February): 2026-02-22 to 2026-02-23 acute_7d_sum 219.4 with coupled ACWR ~0.70 and TSB positive (4.6 to 12.0), showing what a “low-pressure” microcycle looks like in this dataset.

Training status snapshot magnitude: acute_load 629 / chronic_load 503 / ACWR 1.2 (undated) is consistent with the current rolling load magnitude (acute_7d_sum 624.8; chronic_28d_avg 483.0 on 2026-03-09), suggesting the athlete is currently above baseline but not at the extreme spike seen on 2026-03-08.
implications: Acute-load guardrail (near-term): avoid repeating an ~800 acute_7d_sum week immediately (the 2026-03-08 peak). A pragmatic ceiling for the next 7–14 days is keeping acute_7d_sum roughly ≤ ~650–700 unless there is a deliberate overload objective; this corresponds to ~1.3–1.45× the current chronic_28d_avg (483 on 2026-03-09).

Spike indicator limits: for steadier progression, target acwr_uncoupled (EWMA) and acwr_7d28d_uncoupled (rolling sums) to spend most days <~1.6. Values approaching/above ~2.0 (seen 2026-03-02 and 2026-03-08; and strongly on 2026-02-15) indicate acute load is ~2× the baseline from one week earlier.

Ramp-rate limiter: ramp_7d is currently 10.3 (2026-03-09) after peaking at 23.9 (2026-03-08). Keeping ramp_7d generally in the ~0–10 band would reduce repeated “fast-growth” weeks (which align with the highest strain/most negative TSB periods in this dataset).

Monotony/strain limiter: prioritize keeping monotony_7d <~2.0 and strain_7d <~1000 as structural constraints. The dataset’s single largest strain spike (1866.8 on 2026-03-08) was driven by monotony 2.34 plus a very high weekly load; bringing monotony down is the most leverage-efficient way to avoid repeating that peak strain.

Readiness proxy (load-only): when TSB stays below about -25 (e.g., 2026-03-02, 2026-03-04, 2026-03-07, 2026-03-08; and 2026-02-15), you are in the dataset’s highest load-pressure zone. For the 2026-03-28 C race, trending TSB toward neutral (closer to 0) at some point before the event would indicate reduced short-term load pressure (without specifying how to achieve it).
uncertainty: Rolling-window metrics (7d sums, monotony, strain, uncoupled 7d/28d) will remain elevated for several days even if daily load is reduced; interpret changes over multi-day windows, not single days.
No recovery-context metrics (sleep, HRV, subjective readiness) are included here, so guidance is limited to load-shape guardrails rather than a full readiness assessment.
training_status acute/chronic load is undated; it is treated as “current-ish” based on magnitude matching, but exact alignment to a specific day cannot be guaranteed.
